elements.sent-2411-001: Curriculum learning has been a quiet yet crucial component of many of the high-profile successes of reinforcement learning.
elements.sent-2411-002: Despite this, none of the major reinforcement learning libraries directly support curriculum learning or include curriculum learning implementations.
elements.sent-2411-003: These methods can improve the capabilities and robustness of RL agents, but often require significant, complex changes to agent training code.
elements.sent-2411-004: We introduce Syllabus, a library for training RL agents with curriculum learning, as a solution to this problem.
elements.sent-2411-005: Syllabus provides a universal API for curriculum learning algorithms, implementations of popular curriculum learning methods, and infrastructure for easily integrating them with distributed training code written in nearly any RL library.
elements.sent-2411-006: Syllabus provides a minimal API for each of the core components of curriculum learning, dramatically simplifying the process of designing new algorithms and applying existing algorithms to new environments.
elements.sent-2411-007: We demonstrate that the same Syllabus code can be used to train agents written in multiple different RL libraries on numerous domains.
elements.sent-2411-008: In doing so, we present the first examples of curriculum learning in NetHack and Neural MMO, two of the premier challenges for single-agent and multi-agent RL respectively, achieving strong results compared to state of the art baselines.
elements.sent-2411-009: Curricula have been a core component of many of the successes of reinforcement learning (RL).
elements.sent-2411-010: AlphaGo \citep{alphago was trained with self-play, AlphaStar used a novel league training method to achieve grandmaster level play in Starcraft II \citep{Vinyals2019GrandmasterLI , and GT Sophy \citep{gtsophy was taught to outrace professionals in Gran Turismo with manually curated race track sections.}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Curriculum learning is most valuable in environments with large task spaces where, for any given RL agent, many tasks will be too challenging to learn and many will be too easy to provide a useful learning signal.
elements.sent-2411-012: In these settings it is critical to prioritize tasks that teach general skills that can transfer to new tasks and accelerate learning.
elements.sent-2411-013: This problem is further highlighted in environments with infinite or evolving task spaces.
elements.sent-2411-014: Open-endedness research seeks to co-evolve agents and environments in order to create more complex tasks, leading to more complex agent behavior \citep{wang2019paired, wang2020enhanced .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Infinite task spaces only appear in sufficiently complex environments, so this work naturally focuses on the most challenging RL benchmarks available, including Minecraft \citep{guss2019minerl, fan2022minedojo , NetHack \citep{kuttler2020nethack , and Neural MMO \citep{suarez2019neural, rosseau2022toward, suarez2024neural .}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Curriculum learning is an integral component of these open-ended processes, so they directly benefit from better curriculum learning methods.
elements.sent-2411-017: Curriculum learning (CL) methods fit naturally into the RL framework by modifying the data distribution that an agent experiences.
elements.sent-2411-018: According to \citet{narvekar2020curriculum , curriculum learning explores how tasks or data samples can be sequenced to learn problems that can not be solved directly.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Both in theory and in practice, these methods provide orthogonal benefits to policy optimization algorithms and can be combined with those algorithms with minimal restrictions.
elements.sent-2411-020: Despite the near ubiquity of curricula in successful applications of reinforcement learning, there is little support for these methods in standard RL infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-021: This is largely due the variety and complexity of curriculum learning methods.
elements.sent-2411-022: CL algorithms can modify the environment configuration \citep{dennis2020emergent, jiang2021prioritized, jiang2021replay , introduce new neural networks to train \citep{10.5555/3305890.3305962, 10.5555/3305890.3305968, 10.5555/3157096.3157262, henaff2022exploration , or even query tasks from a large language models \citep{zhang2023omni .}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: In practice, these methods are typically implemented in-line with the reinforcement learning algorithm, mixing curriculum learning and reinforcement learning logic together.
elements.sent-2411-024: We argue that this is typically done to simplify the multiprocessing logic.
elements.sent-2411-025: Automatic curriculum learning (ACL) methods maintain a sampling distribution over tasks which they update using feedback from the environment.
elements.sent-2411-026: In a distributed RL setting, this requires additional data to be sent between environments running on separate processes and the curriculum in the main process.
elements.sent-2411-027: The simplest solution is to add these extra messages to the existing message passing infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-028: As a result, many reference implementations of curriculum learning algorithms are written as standalone learning libraries.
elements.sent-2411-029: This entanglement of curriculum learning and reinforcement learning code makes it challenging to use CL methods outside of the codebase where they were introduced inhibiting further research.
elements.sent-2411-030: Syllabus addresses this problem by introducing a simple, portable approach to designing curriculum learning algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-031: Syllabus makes minimal assumptions on the RL training code and creates a separate synchronization pathway which allows us to keep curriculum logic completely independent from the main RL code.
elements.sent-2411-032: We argue that this architecture best enables future research by working with existing RL infrastructure instead of attempting to replace it.
elements.sent-2411-033: We demonstrate the generality of Syllabus by implementing a wide variety of curriculum learning algorithms and using Syllabus to train agents with curriculum learning in CleanRL \citep{huang2022cleanrl , Stable Baselines 3 \citep{stable-baselines3 , RLLib \citep{liang2018rllib , Moolib \citep{moolib2022 , PufferLib \citep{Suarez23:PufferLib on environments including Procgen \citep{pmlr-v119-cobbe20a , NetHack \citep{kuttler2020nethack , and Neural MMO \citep{suarez2019neural, suarez2024neural .}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: Curriculum Learning has been studied in the context of deep supervised learning for many years \citep{10.1145/1553374.1553380, ELMAN199371 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: More recently, it has been studied explicitly as a method to improve the capabilities and generalization of deep reinforcement learning agents.
elements.sent-2411-036: Curriculum learning encompasses a wide range of methods targeted at changing the distribution of data used to train an agent.
elements.sent-2411-037: Their goal is to increase the asymptotic performance or sample efficiency of RL agents on a single environment or range of tasks by sampling tasks that provide maximal learning value.
elements.sent-2411-038: Many diverse methods fall under the broad definition of curriculum learning.
elements.sent-2411-039: Exploration bonuses like curiosity \citep{pathak2017curiosity or novelty \citep{bellemare2016unifying, taiga2021bonus, henaff2022exploration modify the reward function to induce a curriculum by incentivizing the agent to explore unseen section of the state space.}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Methods like self-play \citep{5392560, 10.1145/203330.203343 or league training \citep{Vinyals2019GrandmasterLI create an implicit curriculum by training the opponents in a multiplayer game \citep{leibo2019autocurricula .}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: Progressively more capable opponents lead to progressively more difficult tasks for an agent.
elements.sent-2411-042: Most task-based methods follow the general approach of proposing tasks that are challenging yet solvable, or tasks which the agent is recently performing well on \citep{graves2017automated, kanitscheider2021multi .}
elements.sent-2411-043: In general, curriculum learning methods try to create a sequence of increasingly complex or difficult tasks.
elements.sent-2411-044: Unsupervised Environment Design (UED) is another paradigm for curriculum learning proposed by \citet{dennis2020emergent .}
elements.sent-2411-045: They differentiate UED as a framework in which environments have unspecified parameters, thereby forming an Underspecified Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (UPOMDP).
elements.sent-2411-046: Those parameters are used to produce a distribution of solvable tasks, and the goal of a UED method is to learn a policy that generalizes across a large set of tasks.
elements.sent-2411-047: This approach has led to several algorithms for training agents in procedurally generated games \citep{dennis2020emergent, jiang2021prioritized, jiang2021replay, beukman2024refining, pmlr-v232-mediratta23a .}
elements.sent-2411-048: The specific policy optimization algorithm used to train agents is often independent from the choice of curriculum algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-049: For simplicity, most curriculum learning research and all of the experiments in this paper use Proximal Policy Optimization \citep{schulman2017proximal .}
elements.sent-2411-050: PPO is an on-policy, policy gradient algorithm that has been successfully applied to a wide range of RL environments.
elements.sent-2411-051: It used a clipped objective to avoid large changes to the policy, which prevents collapses in performance and stabilizes learning \citep{pmlr-v37-schulman15, schulman2017proximal .}
elements.sent-2411-052: Mainstream RL libraries do not include curriculum learning algorithms, but there are research libraries for curriculum learning and unsupervised environment design.
elements.sent-2411-053: One of these is the Dual Curriculum Design library \citep{jiang2021replay , which incorporates multiple UED Methods in a single repository.}
elements.sent-2411-054: It includes implementations of PLR \citep{jiang2021prioritized , PAIRED \citep{dennis2020emergent, mediratta2023stabilizing , Robust PLR, REPAIRED \citep{jiang2021replay , and ACCEL \citep{parker2022evolving .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: Watts is another curriculum learning library focused on open-endedness with implementations of POET and PAIRED \citep{dharna2022watts .}
elements.sent-2411-056: It atomizes components of the open-ended framework into modules to create new methods by sharing and combining components from different methods.
elements.sent-2411-057: They also compare algorithms on a suite of common benchmarks and explore different combinations of modules to form new algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-058: Recently, Jax has become a popular choice for writing fast deep learning libraries that can be run end-to-end on hardware accelerators \citep{jax2018github .}
elements.sent-2411-059: Minimax provides Jax-based implementation of the UED algorithms in the DCD library, leading to significantly faster training.
elements.sent-2411-060: JaxUED refactors the UED algorithms in Minimax into single-file implementations for faster prototyping, inspired by CleanRL \citep{huang2022cleanrl .}
elements.sent-2411-061: Both of these libraries are intended to be used with Jax-based environments, which allow you to run the entire training loop on hardware accelerators.
elements.sent-2411-062: However, they provide only moderate performance benefits to the complex CPU-based games that motivate open-endedness research at the cost of inconvenient code constraints.
elements.sent-2411-063: These libraries also suffer from the same lack of portability as previous curriculum learning systems.
elements.sent-2411-064: Syllabus distinguishes itself from previous works by making minimal assumptions on the training code, rather than providing its own training system that intermingles RL and curriculum learning.
elements.sent-2411-065: By defining a simple, uniform API for interfacing with a curriculum learning method, Syllabus makes it possible to add these algorithms to nearly any RL system with minimal code changes.
elements.sent-2411-066: This also means that all of the algorithmic details are contained in a single file, rather than distributed across the algorithm code, task generator, and other infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-067: Finally, Syllabus provides the only general-purpose infrastructure for synchronizing curricula across CPU-based environments in multiple processes.
elements.sent-2411-068: Syllabus aims to simplify the process of developing new curriculum methods, combining them with varying RL algorithms, and applying them to new challenging domains.
elements.sent-2411-069: It is also built to be compatible with many different RL and multiprocessing libraries.
elements.sent-2411-070: These unique goals and restrictions motivate the following key points of our design philosophy:.
elements.sent-2411-071: Syllabus should be agnostic to the choice of reinforcement learning framework.
elements.sent-2411-072: Syllabus should be general enough to support any form of curriculum learning.
elements.sent-2411-073: Integrating Syllabus into training infrastructure should require minimal code changes.
elements.sent-2411-074: When necessary, code complexity should scale with the complexity of the curriculum learning algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-075: Algorithm implementations should be contained in a single file.
elements.sent-2411-076: The first point motivates many of the implementation choices in Syllabus that may seem odd in isolation.
elements.sent-2411-077: To maintain compatibility with several libraries we must honor the Gym environment API \citep{brockman2016openai and write systems that work with many different vectorized environment solutions.}
elements.sent-2411-078: Future adoption of Syllabus will depend heavily on its ability to seamlessly integrate new forms of curriculum learning.
elements.sent-2411-079: The diversity of CL algorithms is highlighted in \autoref{sec:introduction and \autoref{sec:curricula .}}
elements.sent-2411-080: Supporting all of these methods in a single API necessitates some complexity.
elements.sent-2411-081: When one method requires a new interface, we prefer to have a heterogenous, modular API rather than complicate the interface for all methods.
elements.sent-2411-082: For example, we define a separate Curriculum and Agent API for task-based and opponent-based curricula respectively.
elements.sent-2411-083: These components are described in \autoref{sec:curricula and can be used separately or combined to form joint curricula over tasks and opponents.}
elements.sent-2411-084: In each case, we create the smallest possible API surface area to minimize complexity for the end user.
elements.sent-2411-085: Our focus on single-file implementations is inspired by the success of CleanRL \citep{huang2022cleanrl .}
elements.sent-2411-086: CleanRL provides single-file implementations of popular RL algorithms to enable fast iteration while being transparent, making it easy for researchers to reliably report algorithmic details.
elements.sent-2411-087: Though it is impossible to have end-to-end single-file implementations while supporting multiple RL libraries, Syllabus implements all algorithms in a single file to capture many of the same benefits as CleanRL.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth]{images/diagram.png} \caption{Syllabus with a standard distributed RL training setup.} \label{fig:multiprocessing}
elements.sent-2411-088: Syllabus designates responsibility for maintaining sampling distributions over the task space to a separate Curriculum class, and implements task swapping through a TaskWrapper, limiting curriculum code to two locations in the code base.
elements.sent-2411-089: Users can also define a TaskSpace that represents the full range of tasks in an environment that can be used to train agents.
elements.sent-2411-090: These are the main components that users need to modify to develop or apply curriculum learning methods, so each API designed to be simple and general enough to support future use cases.
elements.sent-2411-091: In Syllabus, a Curriculum is responsible for maintaining a distribution over the task space and implementing a sampling function for selecting tasks.
elements.sent-2411-092: Automatic curriculum learning methods require feedback from the RL process to update their sampling distribution.
elements.sent-2411-093: The Curriculum API provides multiple options for receiving updates from different sources.
elements.sent-2411-094: A Curriculum can be manually updated from the main training process, or automatically receive updates through Syllabus's synchronization infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-095: This system will automatically send feedback to the curriculum after each step, episode, or completed task, depending on its requirements.
elements.sent-2411-096: The multiprocessing infrastructure is explained more thoroughly in \autoref{sec:multi .}
elements.sent-2411-097: These update options allow us to implement a diverse range of curriculum learning algorithms under the same API.
elements.sent-2411-098: Since most prior work writes curriculum learning into the training code, our implementations need to be rewritten into this modular framework.
elements.sent-2411-099: To demonstrate the generality of our API, we provide implementations of several popular curriculum learning baselines:.
elements.sent-2411-100: Domain Randomization - uniform sampling of tasks from a discrete set or continuous range, which is a simple yet strong baseline in many domains.
elements.sent-2411-101: Simulated Annealing - a simple curriculum that increases or decreases continuous task values along a predefined range over the course of training \citep{doi:10.1126/science.220.4598.671 .}
elements.sent-2411-102: Prioritized Level Replay - a popular UED method which maintains a buffer of levels with high learning potential \citep{jiang2021prioritized .}
elements.sent-2411-103: PLR uses metrics based on agent value predictions and TD error to prioritize tasks.
elements.sent-2411-104: We generalize this implementation to use arbitrary tasks instead of environment seeds, and implement an asynchronous version that does not depend on synchronized data from training buffers.
elements.sent-2411-105: As a result, our version of PLR can directly receive feedback from the environments.
elements.sent-2411-106: We also implement Robust PLR \citep{jiang2021replay as an initialization option for PLR.}
elements.sent-2411-107: Learning Progress - this method was developed by \citet{kanitscheider2021multi to train PPO agents in Minecraft.}
elements.sent-2411-108: Using this algorithm and an exploration bonus, their agents were able to consistently find diamonds, which PPO alone fails to do.
elements.sent-2411-109: They define a set of binary pass/fail tasks for the agent to achieve, then track a fast and slow exponential moving average (EMA) of the agent's success rate for each task.
elements.sent-2411-110: They calculate the absolute difference between these EMA values and perform several normalization steps to measure the recent change in the agent's performance on each task, which they call learning progress.
elements.sent-2411-111: High learning progress indicates a task that the agent is beginning to learn or forget, so high learning progress tasks are prioritized during sampling.
elements.sent-2411-112: Sequential Curricula - allows users to manually define a sequence of curricula and the stopping conditions for progressing to the next curriculum.
elements.sent-2411-113: It automatically tracks the steps, episodes, and average return across all environments for the current curriculum stage.
elements.sent-2411-114: A subset of the Curriculum API is the Agent API, which allows us to define curricula over co-players in multiagent games.
elements.sent-2411-115: These algorithms store co-players and load them at a later time based on some sampling criteria, enabling advanced multiagent curricula.
elements.sent-2411-116: The currently implemented algorithms are intended for two-player zero-sum games like Chess, but the API itself supports many-agent general-sum games.
elements.sent-2411-117: They are both forms of self-play \citep{5392560, 10.1145/203330.203343 where the opponent is a copy of the online policy.}
elements.sent-2411-118: Fictitious Self Play - trains a single agent and maintains a history of past copies of the agent \citep{10.5555/3045118.3045205 as opponents.}
elements.sent-2411-119: FSP uniformly samples an opponent from this history to prevent strategic cycles that occur in pure self play.
elements.sent-2411-120: Prioritized Fictitious Self Play - like FSP, PFSP maintains a history of past opponents to sample during training.
elements.sent-2411-121: PFSP selects the previous opponent with the highest win-rate against the current agent.
elements.sent-2411-122: This prevents the curriculum from spending a disproportionate amount of time on opponents that the agent already performs well against.
elements.sent-2411-123: Syllabus supports the simultaneous use of opponent-based (e.g. FSP, PFSP) and task-based (e.g. DR, PLR) curricula through the DualCurriculum wrapper.
elements.sent-2411-124: This wrapper extends the Curriculum API, allowing the user to sample from a joint task space and update both curricula at once.
elements.sent-2411-125: This API allows users to experiment with different joint curricula with minimal changes to the training code.
elements.sent-2411-126: Defining the task space of the environment is one of the core design challenges of applying curriculum learning to a new domain.
elements.sent-2411-127: In most benchmark environments, tasks are in a low-dimensional discrete or continuous space.
elements.sent-2411-128: In more challenging environments, the space of possible tasks might be a combination of discrete and continuous variables, or a complex predicate system such as in Neural MMO \citep{suarez2019neural .}
elements.sent-2411-129: Curriculum learning implementations typically only support one specific task space representation.
elements.sent-2411-130: The Task Space API is designed to alleviate the challenges of defining task spaces and identifying which curricula can be used with those spaces.
elements.sent-2411-131: The Task Space API automatically encodes tasks into a Gym Space \citep{brockman2016openai which defines the full range of tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-132: This conversion allows the user to write tasks in the most convenient format for their environment, while using the simplest possible representation within the curriculum code.
elements.sent-2411-133: For instance, Prioritized Level Replay was initially introduced with level seeds as tasks, but our implementation can be applied to any discrete list of tasks.
elements.sent-2411-134: This has the secondary benefit of minimizing the bandwidth required to transfer tasks between processes.
elements.sent-2411-135: We are also able to index the task within the task space and to define a separate training and validation sets.
elements.sent-2411-136: In unsupervised environment design, we study underspecified POMDPs (UPOMDPs), which have free configuration variables that need to be chosen to produce a fully specified POMDP \citep{dennis2020emergent .}
elements.sent-2411-137: In multi-task environments these free variables are the task.
elements.sent-2411-138: Syllabus supports UPOMDPs by accepting a "new\_task" argument in the reset function of the standard Gym API.
elements.sent-2411-139: However, most environments do not support this behavior by default.
elements.sent-2411-140: We provide a TaskWrapper that accepts a new task in its reset function, reconfigures the environment with the new task, then resets the environment for the next episode.
elements.sent-2411-141: This even allows us to add multi-task capabilities to single-task environments.
elements.sent-2411-142: For instance, the NetHack Learning Environment (NLE) introduces several unique objectives such as the Gold, Eat, and Scout tasks alongside the main Score and Challenge environments.
elements.sent-2411-143: Normally swapping tasks requires reinitialization, but we use Syllabus's TaskWrapper to swap between these tasks during resets.
elements.sent-2411-144: You can see the results of training with this setup in \autoref{fig:nethack_sequential .}
elements.sent-2411-145: The Task Interface can also define an optional environment-specific progress metric and encode the current task into the observation space for task-conditional policies.
elements.sent-2411-146: In the simplest case, the progress metric can be a binary value that is 1.0 when the task is completed and 0.0 while it is not.
elements.sent-2411-147: This allows Syllabus to train agents with the Learning Progress curriculum, or other similar methods.
elements.sent-2411-148: As a result of this API structure, Syllabus is by far the easiest curriculum learning library to incorporate with preexisting training code, needing only a few lines of code to incorporate even complex methods like PLR.
elements.sent-2411-149: Swapping between different curricula often requires only a single line of code change.
elements.sent-2411-150: Users can integrate new environments with a simple wrapper that tells Syllabus how to interpret its task space.
elements.sent-2411-151: The next section explains the infrastructure that allows us to maintain this simplicity with distributed RL training infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-152: Syllabus's multiprocessing infrastructure is designed to separate curriculum logic from multiprocessing logic, and provide interoperability with many different forms of distributed RL infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-153: It uses a bidirectional sender-receiver model in which the curriculum sends tasks and the environment sends feedback from playing the provided task.
elements.sent-2411-154: The curriculum synchronization wrapper adds multiprocessing functionality to a Curriculum and an environment synchronization wrapper adds the same functionality to the environment without changing the Gym interface.
elements.sent-2411-155: The environment synchronization wrapper automatically sends feedback to the curriculum after each step, episode, or completed task depending on the curriculum method.
elements.sent-2411-156: You can also update the curriculum with training metrics directly from the main learner process.
elements.sent-2411-157: All updates are batched to reduce multiprocessing overhead, and task sampling is buffered to prevent delays at the start of each episode.
elements.sent-2411-158: Crucially, adding Syllabus's synchronization to existing RL training code requires only a few lines of code, shown in \autoref{fig:code-sample .}
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{minted}[frame=single, linenos]{python} curriculum = DomainRandomization(task_space) curriculum = make_multiprocessing_curriculum(curriculum) env = NetHackScore() env = NetHackTaskWrapper(env) env = MultiProcessingSyncWrapper(env, curriculum.get_components()) \end{minted} \caption{Using Syllabus for curriculum learning with just a few lines of code.} \label{fig:code-sample}
elements.sent-2411-159: The user-facing Curriculum and environment code follows our design goals stated in \autoref{sec:design , while the multiprocessing infrastructure is engineered to ensure stability and reduce the risk of bugs.}
elements.sent-2411-160: To guarantee that researchers will not need to spend time reading or debugging this code, Syllabus includes thorough integration tests, smoke tests, regression tests, and optimization benchmarks for the entire multiprocessing infrastructure, tested with all of the implemented curriculum learning methods.
elements.sent-2411-161: More details and performance numbers can be found in \autoref{app:optimization .}
elements.sent-2411-162: We demonstrate Syllabus's versatility by applying it to strong baselines in four different domains using four separate RL libraries.
elements.sent-2411-163: Each of these libraries has a different design philosophy, software architecture, and multiprocessing layer.
elements.sent-2411-164: Despite this, Syllabus can be easily applied to all of them with only a few lines of library-agnostic code.
elements.sent-2411-165: We provide full details for these experiments in \autoref{app:experiments and a summary of the results in \autoref{sec:results .}}
elements.sent-2411-166: The data for all of these experiments is publicly available on Weights \& Biases and the code is available at \href{https://github.com/RyanNavillus/Syllabus {github.com/RyanNavillus/Syllabus .}}
elements.sent-2411-167: Cart Pole in RLLib - Cart Pole is a simple toy environment mainly used to debug RL implementations \citep{6313077 .}
elements.sent-2411-168: It initializes a cart to a random starting point along a 2D track and tasks the agent with balancing a pole for as long as possible.
elements.sent-2411-169: It is not a multitask environment, so we use Syllabus's task wrapper to make the cart's initialization range a configurable option.
elements.sent-2411-170: This experiment demonstrates how Syllabus's Task Interface can add multitask functionality to singleton environments, and that Syllabus integrates easily with RLLib's Ray-based multiprocessing infrastructure \citep{moritz2018ray, liang2018rllib .}
elements.sent-2411-171: Procgen in CleanRL - Procgen is a collection of procedurally generated arcade game environments designed to test the generalization of RL agents \citep{pmlr-v119-cobbe20a .}
elements.sent-2411-172: In this environment, we use curriculum learning to select the environment seed for each episode, following prior work \citep{jiang2021prioritized .}
elements.sent-2411-173: CleanRL provides a single-file implementation of PPO, which allows direct access to training code and makes integrating Syllabus trivial.
elements.sent-2411-174: We also use Procgen to validate our PLR implementation by comparing directly to the original implementation by \citet{jiang2021prioritized .}
elements.sent-2411-175: Neural MMO in PufferLib - Neural MMO 2.0 is a complex multi-agent simulation inspired by massively multiplayer online games \citep{suarez2019neural, suarez2024neural .}
elements.sent-2411-176: Agents can collect resources, learn skills, trade goods, and fight non-player characters or other agents.
elements.sent-2411-177: It has a predicate task space which allows users to define objectives in Python code.
elements.sent-2411-178: The baseline for the 2023 Neural MMO competition was written in PufferLib because it supports complex action spaces and multiagent environments where agents can die, which complicates learning code \citep{terry2021pettingzoo, suarez2024neural .}
elements.sent-2411-179: We show that Syllabus can be used in this environment with 128 agents and a massive task space.
elements.sent-2411-180: NetHack in Moolib - NetHack is a popular text-based dungeon-crawler released in 1987, and adapted into an RL environment by \citet{kuttler2020nethack .}
elements.sent-2411-181: It's a complex, procedurally generated game in which winning or "ascending" can take more than 50,000 steps for human players.
elements.sent-2411-182: Ascending requires players to solve puzzles using common sense, knowledge of mythology, and game-specific tricks, all while collecting equipment, fighting monsters, and scavenging for food.
elements.sent-2411-183: NetHack remains one of the hardest benchmarks for online RL methods, which lag behind hand-crafted symbolic agents and behavior cloning baselines \citep{kuttler2020nethack, tuyls2023scaling, piterbarg2024nethack .}
elements.sent-2411-184: Importantly, NetHack can be simulated extremely quickly, shifting the training bottleneck from data collection to policy optimization and inference.
elements.sent-2411-185: To address this, NetHack baselines use specialized training libraries like TorchBeast \citep{kuttler2019torchbeast , Sample Factory \citep{petrenko2020sample , and MooLib \citep{moolib2022 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-186: SampleFactory and Moolib use asynchronous PPO (APPO), which create separate copies of the policy for action inference and optimization.
elements.sent-2411-187: Moolib in particular creates servers for policies and environments and communicates via remote procedure calls (RPCs), allowing it to scale to many machines and GPUs.
elements.sent-2411-188: We demonstrate that Syllabus is easy to use with Moolib in a single GPU setting.
elements.sent-2411-189: In all environments except CartPole, we choose to compare Domain Randomization against Prioritized Level Replay, because PLR uses the full range of Syllabus's multiprocessing infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-190: For Neural MMO, we create a single shared replay buffer of level seeds and update it using data from all 128 agents, which are controlled by a single self-play agent.

elements.sent-2411-212: Multi-domain research is crucial to developing generalizable and robust RL methods.
elements.sent-2411-213: Curriculum learning is most valuable in challenging, open-ended environments that approach the complexity of the real world.
elements.sent-2411-214: In practice, the complexity of these domains necessitates custom learning infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-215: NetHack agents are trained in libraries that are specially designed to maximize hardware utilization, taking advantage of NetHack's fast simulation speed.
elements.sent-2411-216: Neural MMO agents are trained in PufferLib because it has native support and optimizations for the complexities of multiagent environments.
elements.sent-2411-217: Syllabus is designed for this reality of training agents in complex environments.
elements.sent-2411-218: Isolating curriculum learning code from reinforcement learning code allows us to apply curriculum learning to complex environments without developing or adapting new infrastructure for each environment.
elements.sent-2411-219: We hope this will improve the reproducibility of curriculum learning research and help to push it away from toy environments and toward domains that challenge modern RL methods.
elements.sent-2411-220: Much of the recent infrastructure work in unsupervised environment design has been written in Jax.
elements.sent-2411-221: Jax allows environments and training code to be parallelized on hardware accelerators, producing experimental results hundreds of times faster than equivalent CPU-based environments.
elements.sent-2411-222: Jax is a powerful tool for conducting fast research, but it also enforces strict requirements on how code is written, slowing down development, and incentivizing simplistic environments.
elements.sent-2411-223: For this reason, it will be challenging for Jax-based simulations to reach the complexity of even CPU-based research environments, much less the professionally developed videogames that have historically been benchmarks for reinforcement learning.
elements.sent-2411-224: Jax has empowered curriculum learning research and led to new discoveries using simpler environments, and we hope that Syllabus will similarly enable curriculum learning in complex domains.
elements.sent-2411-225: Syllabus is under continuous development as we add more features, benchmark more methods, and provide support for more general approaches to curriculum learning.
elements.sent-2411-226: In the near future, we plan to implement more open-ended algorithms including PAIRED \citep{dennis2020emergent and OMNI \citep{zhang2023omni .}}
elements.sent-2411-227: We also plan to take advantage of Syllabus's portability to develop a comprehensive benchmark for curriculum learning methods using the reference implementations of the strongest available baselines in a range of environments..
elements.figure-2411-005: \label{listing:curriculum} \begin{minted}[frame=single, linenos, fontfamily=cmss]{python} class Curriculum: """API for defining curricula to interface with Gym environments.""" def _sample_distribution(self) -> List[float]: """ Returns a sample distribution over the task space. Any curriculum that maintains a true probability distribution should implement this method to retrieve the distribution. """ def sample(self, k: int = 1) -> List[Any]: """ Sample k tasks from the curriculum. """ def update_task_progress(self, task: Any, progress: Tuple[float, bool]) """ Update the curriculum with a task and its progress. Progress is defined by the environment's TaskWrapper. """ def update_on_step(self, obs: Any, rew: float, done: bool, info: dict): """ Update the curriculum with the environment outputs for the most recent step. """ def update_on_episode(self, return: float, length: int, task: Any, env_id: int = None): """Update the curriculum with episode results from the environment.""" def update_on_demand(self, metrics: Dict): """ Update the curriculum with arbitrary inputs. Typically used to incorporate gradient or error-based metrics from the training process. """ def get_opponent(self, opponent_id: int) -> Agent: """ Load the agent corresponding to the given opponent_id. """ def update_agent(self, opponent: Agent): """ Add opponent to agent store. """ def update_winrate(self, opponent_id: int, opponent_return: int): """ Update the winrate for the given opponent_id based on environment returns. """ \end{minted} \caption{An abbreviated summary of the Curriculum interface. These represent the main methods for updating and sampling from a curriculum in Syllabus. The get\_opponent, update\_agent, and update\_winrate methods are used for self-play.}

The task will be passed to the environment in this format.""" def encode(self, task: Any) -> Any: """ Convert the task to an efficient encoding that can be interpreted by the curriculum. elements.sent-2411-228: As a consequence of the choice to use a separate multiprocessing system from the RL training loop, Syllabus incurs some unavoidable computational costs.
elements.sent-2411-229: Specifically, receiving and sending information in the environments decreases the effective steps per second of each environment, while sampling and sending tasks in the actor process increases the computational load on the main process.
elements.sent-2411-230: We perform experiments on the NetHack Learning Environment \citep{kuttler2020nethack to demonstrate the effect of this choice on overall steps per second.}
elements.sent-2411-231: We evaluate with a minimal curriculum that always returns the same task to isolate the impact of our multiprocessing infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-232: The results are shown in \autoref{tab:performance for 128 environments each running 64 episodes on a 2.20GHz 32-core Intel i9-13950HX \footnote{Syllabus is under continuous development, so these numbers may not reflect the performance of the most recent version of the library.}}
elements.sent-2411-233: They are accurate as of the time of publication.. .
elements.sent-2411-234: We test Syllabus using both Python's native multiprocessing package and Ray \citep{moritz2018ray .}
elements.sent-2411-235: Syllabus allows you to disable per-step updates for curricula that do not require them, instead only sending updates and new tasks at the end of each episode.
elements.sent-2411-236: Note that this is a worst case test for several reasons.
elements.sent-2411-237: Typically in distributed reinforcement learning, environments are vectorized and stepped together, such that N environments step at the speed of the single slowest environment.
elements.sent-2411-238: Here we run each environment independently, so they are not bottlenecked by vectorization.
elements.sent-2411-239: The NLE is an extremely fast environment with large observations, which stresses the multiprocessing communication bandwidth.
elements.sent-2411-240: Finally, RL training with larger architectures is usually bottle-necked by policy optimization rather than environment iteration time.
elements.sent-2411-241: We expect Syllabus's impact on performance (as a percentage of total computation) to be much lower for more computationally intensive environments and when used in a real RL training context.

elements.figure-2411-016: \caption{Adding curriculum learning with Syllabus to RLLib training code with just a few lines of code.} \label{app:rllib} \begin{minted}[frame=single, linenos]{diff} import gym from ray.tune.registry import register_env from ray import tune from gym.spaces import Box from .task_wrappers import CartPoleTaskWrapper + from syllabus.core import RaySyncWrapper, make_ray_curriculum + from syllabus.curricula import SimpleBoxCurriculum + from syllabus.task_space import TaskSpace if __name__ == "__main__": + # Define a task space + task_space = TaskSpace(Box(-0.3, 0.3, shape=(2,))) def env_creator(config): env = gym.make("CartPole-v1") # Wrap the environment to change tasks on reset() env = CartPoleTaskWrapper(env) + # Add environment sync wrapper + env = RaySyncWrapper( + env, default_task=(-0.02, 0.02), task_space=task_space + ) return env register_env("task_cartpole", env_creator) + # Create the curriculum + curriculum = SimpleBoxCurriculum(task_space) + # Add the curriculum sync wrapper + curriculum = make_ray_curriculum(curriculum) config = { "env": "task_cartpole", "num_gpus": 1, "num_workers": 8, "framework": "torch", } tuner = tune.Tuner("APEX", param_space=config) results = tuner.fit() \end{minted}

elements.sent-2411-309: However, because Syllabus makes minimal modifications to the training code, we expect that using those methods alongside Syllabus will be no harder than using them alone.
elements.sent-2411-310: Curriculum learning has been a quiet, yet crucial component of many high-profile successes of reinforcement learning.
elements.sent-2411-311: Despite this, it is still a niche topic that is not directly supported by any of the major reinforcement learning libraries.
elements.sent-2411-312: These methods can improve the capabilities and generalization of RL agents, but often require complex changes to training code.
elements.sent-2411-313: We introduce Syllabus, a portable curriculum learning library, as a solution to this problem.
elements.sent-2411-314: Syllabus provides a universal API for curriculum learning, modular implementations of popular automatic curriculum learning methods, and infrastructure that allows them to be easily integrated with asynchronous training code in nearly any RL library.
elements.sent-2411-315: Syllabus provides a minimal API for core curriculum learning components, making it easier to design new algorithms and adapt existing ones to new environments.
elements.sent-2411-316: We demonstrate this by evaluating the algorithms in Syllabus on several new environments, each using agents written in a different RL library.
elements.sent-2411-317: We present the first examples of automatic curriculum learning in NetHack and Neural MMO, two of the most challenging RL benchmarks, and find evidence that existing methods do not directly transfer to complex new environments.
elements.sent-2411-319: AlphaGo \citep{alphago was trained with self-play, AlphaStar used a novel league training method to achieve grandmaster level play in Starcraft II \citep{Vinyals2019GrandmasterLI , and GT Sophy \citep{gtsophy was taught to outrace professionals in Gran Turismo with manually curated sections of race tracks.}}}
elements.sent-2411-320: Curriculum learning is essential in environments with large task spaces where many tasks are too challenging or too easy to provide a useful learning signal.
elements.sent-2411-321: In these settings agents must prioritize tasks that teach transferable skills to accelerate learning in new tasks.
elements.sent-2411-322: This problem is especially pronounced in open-ended environments with infinite or evolving task spaces, similar to the real world.
elements.sent-2411-323: Open-endedness research seeks to co-evolve agents and environments in order to create more complex tasks and incentivize more intelligent agent behavior \citep{wang2019paired, wang2020enhanced .}

elements.sent-2411-327: In theory, CL provides orthogonal benefits to policy optimization algorithms and they can be integrated with minimal restrictions.

elements.sent-2411-331: Most open-source implementations of CL algorithms integrate their code directly within the RL algorithm, intertwining CL and RL logic.
elements.sent-2411-332: We argue that this is typically done to accommodate existing multiprocessing implementations.
elements.sent-2411-333: ACL methods maintain a sampling distribution over tasks which they update using feedback from the environment.
elements.sent-2411-334: In an asynchronous RL setting, this requires additional data exchanges between environments running on separate processes and the curriculum in the main process.
elements.sent-2411-335: The simplest solution is to add these extra messages to the existing message-passing infrastructure, usually in the infos dictionary.
elements.sent-2411-336: However, accessing this data requires direct changes to the training code that vary depending on the CL method, so CL algorithms are implemented as standalone libraries with custom training code.
elements.sent-2411-337: This entanglement of CL and RL code makes it difficult to isolate algorithmic details and apply CL methods beyond their original codebases, ultimately limiting reproducibility and hindering future research.
elements.sent-2411-338: We implement CL algorithms as modular additions to RL code, complementing their theoretical orthogonality to policy optimization.
elements.sent-2411-339: Syllabus makes minimal assumptions about the RL training code and establishes a separate synchronization pathway between the curriculum and asynchronous environments.

elements.sent-2411-373: Syllabus distinguishes itself from previous works by making minimal assumptions on the training code, rather than providing its own training system that intermingles RL and CL.
elements.sent-2411-374: By defining a simple, uniform API for interfacing with a curriculum, Syllabus makes it possible to add these algorithms to nearly any RL system with minimal code changes.
elements.sent-2411-375: An additional benefit of this approach is that all of the algorithmic details are contained in a single place, rather than distributed across the codebase.
elements.sent-2411-376: Syllabus also provides the only general-purpose infrastructure for synchronizing curricula across CPU-based environments in multiple processes.
elements.sent-2411-377: Curriculum learning algorithms almost exclusively interact with environments rather than policy optimization, and Syllabus takes advantage of this through a unique paradigm of portable infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-378: Syllabus aims to simplify the process of developing curriculum learning methods, combining them with RL algorithms, and applying them to challenging domains.
elements.sent-2411-379: As such, it is built to be compatible with as many different RL libraries and multiprocessing methods as possible.
elements.sent-2411-380: These goals motivate the following key points of our design philosophy:.
elements.sent-2411-381: Syllabus should have general APIs that support any form of curriculum learning.
elements.sent-2411-382: Code complexity should scale with the complexity of the curriculum learning algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-383: To maintain compatibility with several libraries we must honor the Gymnasium and PettingZoo environment APIs \citep{brockman2016openai, towers2024gymnasium, terry2021pettingzoo and write systems that work with many different multiprocessing solutions.}
elements.sent-2411-384: For instance, Gymnasium, Stable Baselines 3 \citep{stable-baselines3 , and RLLib \citep{liang2018rllib all provide their own vector environment implementations.}}
elements.sent-2411-385: Syllabus's utility is tied to its ability to integrate new forms of curriculum learning.
elements.sent-2411-386: The diversity of existing CL algorithms is highlighted in \autoref{sec:introduction and \autoref{sec:curricula .}}
elements.sent-2411-387: When one method requires a new interface, we prefer to have a heterogeneous, modular API rather than complicate the interface for all methods.
elements.sent-2411-388: In each case, we create the smallest possible API to minimize complexity for users.
elements.sent-2411-389: CleanRL provides single-file implementations of popular RL algorithms to support fast iteration and transparency, making it easy for researchers to reliably report algorithmic details.
elements.sent-2411-390: Though end-to-end single-file training scripts are inherently non-portable, Syllabus encapsulates all CL logic for each algorithm in a single class to capture much of the same transparency and simplicity.

elements.sent-2411-391: Syllabus designates responsibility for maintaining sampling distributions over the task space to a Curriculum class, and implements task swapping through a wrapper over the environment.
elements.sent-2411-392: This provides a uniform interface for setting the current task of an environment, which we explain fully in \autoref{app:task_api .}
elements.sent-2411-393: Each environment also defines a TaskSpace that represents the full range of tasks that can be used to train agents.
elements.sent-2411-394: Each API is designed to be simple to use and to support future use cases.
elements.sent-2411-395: Automatic curriculum learning methods use feedback from the RL training process to update their sampling distribution.
elements.sent-2411-396: The Curriculum API provides multiple options for incorporating information from the environments and policy, either manually or automatically through Syllabus's synchronization infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-397: The multiprocessing approach is explained more thoroughly in \autoref{sec:multi .}
elements.sent-2411-398: The curriculum can be updated after each step, episode, or completed task with the update\_on\_step, update\_on\_episode, or update\_task\_progress methods respectively.
elements.sent-2411-399: These update options allow us to implement a diverse range of curriculum learning algorithms under the same API, as we show in \autoref{sec:implementations .}
elements.sent-2411-400: The Agent API is a subset of the Curriculum API that defines curricula over co-players in multi-agent games.
elements.sent-2411-401: These algorithms store co-players and load them at a later time based on some sampling criteria.
elements.sent-2411-402: The currently implemented algorithms are forms of self-play (SP) \citep{5392560, 10.1145/203330.203343 where the opponent is a previous copy of the online policy.}
elements.sent-2411-403: These are intended for two-player competitive games, but the API supports many-agent general-sum games.
elements.sent-2411-404: Fictitious Self Play (FSP) - maintains a history of past copies of the agent as opponents \citep{10.5555/3045118.3045205 .}
elements.sent-2411-405: FSP uniformly samples an opponent from history to prevent strategic cycles.
elements.sent-2411-406: Prioritized Fictitious Self Play (PFSP) - like FSP, PFSP maintains a history of past opponents to sample during training.
elements.sent-2411-407: PFSP selects the opponent with the highest win-rate against the current agent.
elements.sent-2411-408: This prevents the curriculum from spending a disproportionate amount of time training against opponents that the agent already performs well against.
elements.sent-2411-409: Syllabus supports the simultaneous use of opponent-based (e.g. FSP, PFSP) and task-based (e.g. DR, PLR) curricula through the DualCurriculumWrapper.
elements.sent-2411-410: This API allows users to experiment with different joint curricula without changing their training code.
elements.sent-2411-411: Defining a task space for training agents is a fundamental challenge in curriculum learning, similar to reward shaping for policy optimization.
elements.sent-2411-412: Task spaces are domain-specific and must be carefully designed for each environment to create an effective curricula.
elements.sent-2411-413: In most benchmark environments, tasks exist in a low-dimensional discrete or continuous space, while more complex environments may use a combination of discrete and continuous variables or intricate predicate systems, as seen in XLand \citep{team2023human, nikulin2023xlandminigrid and Neural MMO \citep{suarez2019neural .}}
elements.sent-2411-414: Curriculum learning implementations are often restricted to the task space representations from the environments that they were originally designed for, but Syllabus's Task API removes these limitations, making it possible to explore new task spaces without modifying algorithm code.
elements.sent-2411-415: The Task Space API simplifies task space definition and curriculum compatibility by mapping tasks into a Gym Space \citep{brockman2016openai .}
elements.sent-2411-416: This allows users to define tasks in a format suited to their environment while maintaining a simple representation within the curriculum code.
elements.sent-2411-417: For example, while Prioritized Level Replay originally used level seeds as tasks, our implementation supports any discrete task list.
elements.sent-2411-418: The PLR code handles tasks as integers for streamlined algorithm logic, while the environment interprets them as seeds, map encodings, reward functions, etc. This approach also reduces bandwidth for task transfer between processes and enables task indexing for defining separate training and validation sets.

elements.sent-2411-419: The real practical challenges in curriculum learning come from synchronizing curricula using feedback from environments running in multiple processes.
elements.sent-2411-420: Syllabus's infrastructure is designed to separate curriculum and multiprocessing logic to provide interoperability with many different forms of asynchronous RL infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-421: The curriculum does not need immediate feedback or block training, so all updates are batched to reduce multiprocessing overhead, and task sampling is can be buffered to prevent delays at the start of each episode.
elements.sent-2411-422: The user-facing curriculum and environment code follows our design goals stated in \autoref{sec:design , while the multiprocessing infrastructure is engineered to ensure stability and reduce the risk of bugs.}
elements.sent-2411-423: To guarantee that researchers will not need to spend time reading or debugging this code, Syllabus includes thorough integration tests, smoke tests, regression tests, and optimization benchmarks for all multiprocessing code, tested with all of the implemented curriculum learning methods.
elements.sent-2411-424: More details and performance numbers can be found in \autoref{app:testing and \autoref{app:optimization .}}
elements.sent-2411-425: Syllabus includes portable implementations of several popular curriculum learning baselines.
elements.sent-2411-426: We prioritize modern methods which have been successfully applied to complex environments, and plan to add many more algorithms in future versions of Syllabus.
elements.sent-2411-427: We also provide utilities for manual curriculum learning or transfer learning.
elements.sent-2411-428: For example, we implement Simulated Annealing \citep{doi:10.1126/science.220.4598.671 , an expanding sampling range curriculum, and a sequential curriculum.}
elements.sent-2411-429: The sequentual curricula trains agents on a list of individual tasks or entire curricula in stages that run until a predetermined number of steps or user-defined stopping conditions are met.
elements.sent-2411-430: Supporting all of these diverse methods helps to demonstrate the generality of Syllabus's interfaces.
elements.sent-2411-431: Prioritized Level Replay (PLR) - a popular UED method which maintains a buffer of levels with high learning potential \citep{jiang2021prioritized .}
elements.sent-2411-432: PLR typically prioritizes tasks with a high value loss, though recent work explores other options \citep{jiang2021replay, jackson2024discovering .}
elements.sent-2411-433: It also tracks the staleness of each task's value loss score, and assigns some probability to very stale tasks.
elements.sent-2411-434: We generalize this implementation to support arbitrary task spaces instead of only environment seeds, and implement an asynchronous version that can be updated with arbitrary batch sizes.
elements.sent-2411-435: PLR is a core component of several more recent UED methods \citep{parker2022evolving, samvelyan2022maestro and has been successfully applied to complex domains \citep{team2023human .}}
elements.sent-2411-436: We sample tasks based on the L1 value loss as defined below, where $\lambda$ and $\gamma$ are the GAE and MDP discount factors respectively, and $\delta t$ is the TD-error at timestep $t$: \begin{equation} \label{eq:pvl} \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=0}^{T} \left|\sum_{k=t}^T(\gamma\lambda)^{k-t}\delta_k\right|\end{equation} Learning Progress (LP) - this method was developed by \citet{kanitscheider2021multi to train PPO agents in Minecraft.}
elements.sent-2411-437: Using this algorithm and an exploration bonus, their agents were able to consistently acquire diamonds, drastically surpassing PPO alone.
elements.sent-2411-438: They define a set of pass/fail tasks for the agent to achieve, and prioritize tasks with a recent change in success rate, which they call learning progress.
elements.sent-2411-439: This method tracks a fast and slow exponential moving average (EMA) of the agent's success rate for each task, which is evaluated periodically throughout training.
elements.sent-2411-440: Learning progress is calculated by taking the absolute difference between these EMA values and performing several normalization steps.
elements.sent-2411-441: High learning progress indicates a task that the agent is beginning to learn or forget, so those tasks are prioritized during sampling.
elements.sent-2411-442: Open-endedness via Models of human Notions of Interestingness (OMNI) - this is an extension to the Learning Progress curriculum introduced by \citep{zhang2023omni which asks an LLM to identify "interesting" tasks given the agent's current success rates on each task.}
elements.sent-2411-443: The LLM filters out tasks that are uninteresting given high proficiency at another task.
elements.sent-2411-444: Starting with the highest success rate, the LLM partitions tasks into interesting and uninteresting sets, and masks uninteresting tasks out of the sampling distribution generated by the LP curriculum.
elements.sent-2411-445: This approach has recently been extended to allow the LLM to generate new interesting tasks defined in code \citep{faldor2024omni .}
elements.sent-2411-446: Sampling for Learnability (SFL) - this method is a hybrid of the approaches used by PLR and LP.
elements.sent-2411-447: It uses a simple metric for prioritizing tasks by sampling according to $p(1-p)$ where $p$ is the task success rate, a metric called learnability that was originally described in \citep{tzannetos2023proximal .}
elements.sent-2411-448: The method as introduced by \citet{rutherfordno samples from a mixture of a uniform distribution over the top $k$ most learnable tasks and a uniform distribution over all tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-449: The mixing ratio of these distributions is controlled by a hyperparameter $p$.
elements.sent-2411-450: We additionally implement a version of this method that samples directly from the full distribution generated by $p(1-p)$.
elements.sent-2411-451: We find that it sometimes outperforms SFL while removing two environment-dependent hyperparameters.

elements.sent-2411-523: These results suggest that curricula over initial environment conditions are not effective in complex, long-horizon games, and that we may need to develop more sophisticated methods for these environments.
elements.sent-2411-524: Multi-domain research, focusing on challenging, long-horizon environments, is crucial to developing generalizable and robust RL methods.
elements.sent-2411-525: This makes them inherently difficult yet learnable while being compelling and interpretable.
elements.sent-2411-526: In practice, solving these complex problems with reinforcement learning often requires custom infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-527: NetHack agents are trained in libraries that can take advantage of its fast simulation speed.
elements.sent-2411-528: Neural MMO agents are built with PufferLib because it natively supports the complexities of multi-agent interactions.
elements.sent-2411-529: Separating curriculum learning from RL code allows us to apply the same methods to new environments without re-implementation.
elements.sent-2411-530: We hope this will improve the reproducibility of curriculum learning research and help to push the field away from toy environments and toward domains that challenge modern RL methods.
elements.sent-2411-531: Much of the recent UED infrastructure has been written in JAX.
elements.sent-2411-532: This allows environments and training code to be parallelized on hardware accelerators, producing experimental results hundreds of times faster than equivalent CPU-based environments.
elements.sent-2411-533: JAX is a powerful tool for conducting fast research, but it also enforces strict requirements on how code is written, slowing development and incentivizing simplistic environments.
elements.sent-2411-534: It will be challenging for JAX-based simulations to reach the complexity of CPU-based research environments, much less the professionally developed video games that have historically been benchmarks for reinforcement learning.
elements.sent-2411-535: Syllabus is designed to extend the benefits of curriculum learning to more complex domains.
elements.sent-2411-536: Our results suggest that although curriculum learning is effective in moderately challenging single-agent environments like Procgen and Crafter, standard approaches may not be sufficient for complex, long-horizon, and multi-agent environments like NetHack and Neural MMO.
elements.sent-2411-537: These results could likely be improved by using curricula over more meaningful axes of the task space, such as reward functions in NetHack or opponent strategies in Neural MMO.
elements.sent-2411-538: However, more complex task spaces also introduce new challenges.
elements.sent-2411-539: For instance, training on multiple reward functions will require careful per-task reward normalization.
elements.sent-2411-540: One practical finding from our experiments is that SFL performs at least as well as LP in each environment, and only scores lower than DR in NetHack.
elements.sent-2411-541: In addition, our full distribution variant of SFL performs comparably to the original top K implementation and requires no hyperparameter tuning.
elements.sent-2411-542: Therefore, our full distribution implementation of SFL is a strong initial choice for exploring curriculum learning on new domains.
elements.sent-2411-543: Syllabus is actively evolving as we add features, benchmark new methods, and expand support for general curriculum learning approaches.
elements.sent-2411-544: We hope it enables future research and encourages evaluation on more challenging domains.
elements.sent-2411-545: We hope it serves as a foundation for future curriculum learning research, particularly in challenging environments.
elements.sent-2411-546: Syllabus defines a separate multiprocessing pathway to send data to the curriculum.
elements.sent-2411-547: When curricula require standard environment outputs, this will send the same information as the RL training pathway, potentially leading to bandwidth or processing bottlenecks.
elements.sent-2411-548: It is possible to avoid this duplication by writing additional code to forward data from the training process to the curriculum.
elements.sent-2411-549: Syllabus does not support distributed environment servers, though it is possible via RPC calls with minimal modification.
elements.sent-2411-550: Syllabus's multiprocessing infrastructure cannot automatically communicate with environments vectorized in JAX or C without additional code, though the curricula can still be used directly.
elements.sent-2411-551: As a result, it can not be used in fully parallel training systems.
elements.sent-2411-552: Syllabus does not currently include methods that train additional networks or create new reward components.
elements.sent-2411-553: However, using those methods alongside Syllabus will be no harder than using them without Syllabus.

elements.sent-2411-565: Syllabus implements a Sequential curriculum, which can be though of as a meta-curriculum over individual Curriculum objects.
elements.sent-2411-566: The curriculum has multiple stages, each of which will run until user-defined stopping conditions are met, after which the curriculum will move on to the next stage.
elements.sent-2411-567: The curriculum passes any updates that it receives to the current curriculum stage, so we can even use automatic curricula sequentially.
elements.sent-2411-568: It is initialized with a set of stages, which can be individual elements of the task space, lists of tasks, entire task spaces, or other Curriculum objects.
elements.sent-2411-569: Individual tasks will be converted into a Constant curriculum which always returns the same task.
elements.sent-2411-570: Lists of tasks or task spaces are converted to DomainRandomization curricula over the provided tasks.
elements.sent-2411-571: The stopping conditions can be predefined numbers of steps, tasks, or episodes, or they can be episodic return thresholds.
elements.sent-2411-572: We use a convenient text-based interface for defining stopping conditions which supports composite conditions.
elements.sent-2411-573: For example, the user can pass "return>=1.0\&\&episodes>=1000" as a valid composite conditions, which will stop the current stage once the agent has experiences 1000 episodes and achieves at least 1.0 return.
elements.sent-2411-574: The sequential curriculum receives updates from the environments to track agent progress automatically.
elements.sent-2411-575: For an example of this sequential curriculum in practice, see \autoref{app:nmmo-sequential .}
elements.sent-2411-576: We evaluate Syllabus with NetHack to demonstrate the effect on overall steps per second.
elements.sent-2411-577: We use a minimal curriculum that always returns the same task to isolate the impact of our multiprocessing infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-578: Syllabus skips per-step updates for curricula that do not require them, instead only sending episodic data and tasks at the end of each episode.
elements.sent-2411-579: Typically in asynchronous reinforcement learning, environments are vectorized and stepped together, such that N environments step at the speed of the single slowest environment.
elements.sent-2411-580: Finally, RL is usually bottle-necked by policy optimization rather than environment iteration time.

elements.sent-2411-635: Phasic Policy Gradients (PPG) \citep{cobbe2021phasic is an extension of PPO that trains a separate value and policy network, while distilling features from the value network into the actor.}
elements.sent-2411-636: They do this by adding an auxiliary value head to the policy, and periodically using behavior cloning from the value network into the auxiliary value head, allowing the actor to learn features used critic.
elements.sent-2411-637: We use Syllabus study whether Prioritized Level Replay can provide an additional level of improvement over PPG as it does for PPO.
elements.sent-2411-638: PPG updates the policy and value with a separate number of epochs, but by default uses 1 value epoch and 1 policy epoch.
elements.sent-2411-639: We see in \autoref{fig:ppg that applying PLR to PPG with the default hyperparameters performs worse than DR, despite its close similarity to PPO.}
elements.sent-2411-640: We hypothesize that this is may be due to the lack of value updates.
elements.sent-2411-641: If the value predictions are less accurate, then PLR's score will also be inaccurate.
elements.sent-2411-642: We further investigate increasing the number of value epochs, and find that by increasing the number of value epochs to 3, the same as PPO, PLR matches but does not exceed Dr..

elements.figure-2411-037: \caption{Adding curriculum learning with Syllabus to RLLib training code with just a few lines of code.} \label{app:rllib} \begin{minted}[frame=single, linenos]{diff} import gym from ray.tune.registry import register_env from ray import tune from gym.spaces import Box from .task_wrappers import CartPoleTaskWrapper + from syllabus.core import RaySyncWrapper, make_ray_curriculum + from syllabus.curricula import ExpandingBox + from syllabus.task_space import BoxTaskSpace if __name__ == "__main__": + # Define a task space + task_space = BoxTaskSpace(Box(-0.3, 0.3, shape=(2,))) def env_creator(config): env = gym.make("CartPole-v1") + # Wrap the environment to change tasks on reset() + env = CartPoleTaskWrapper(env) + # Add environment sync wrapper + env = RaySyncWrapper(env, task_space) return env register_env("task_cartpole", env_creator) + # Create the curriculum + curriculum = ExpandingBox(task_space) + # Add the curriculum sync wrapper + curriculum = make_ray_curriculum(curriculum) config = { "env": "task_cartpole", "num_gpus": 1, "num_workers": 8, "framework": "torch", } tuner = tune.Tuner("APEX", param_space=config) results = tuner.fit() \end{minted}
